topic: Describing Emotions and Figurative Language Review
title: Describing Emotions and Figurative Language Review
simple_explanation: This page is like a word treasure hunt! We find lots of different words to say if someone is happy or grumpy. We also remember our poetry tricks like words that start with the same sound (alliteration) or words that rhyme. And we learn about 'idioms,' which are secret code phrases like 'it's raining cats and dogs' – it doesn't mean animals are falling, just that it's raining a lot!
detailed_explanation: Remember those cool poetic tricks? 'Alliteration' is when words start with the same sound, making a fun rhythm. 'Rhyme' is when words at the end of lines sound similar, and 'personification' is when you give human actions to things that aren't human. We also have 'idioms'. These are special phrases where the words don't mean exactly what they say. For example, 'every cloud has a silver lining' doesn't mean actual clouds, but that there's always something good to find even in a tough situation. Understanding these language tools helps you write more expressively and also appreciate the clever ways poets like Grace Nichols (who wrote our 'Sun is laughing' poem!) choose their words.

examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of describing emotions and figurative language review in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: Idiom: If you're having a bad day, a friend might say, 'Don't worry, *every cloud has a silver lining*,' meaning things will get better.
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of describing emotions and figurative language review in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: Personification: 'The *storm seized the sky* and *tore it to shreds*.'
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of describing emotions and figurative language review in a real sentence.

guided_practice: Identify the meaning of 'blithe' in a sentence. Remember it means: very happy and cheerful.
Match the word 'exuberant' with a similar concept from your own experience.
Look at the first example again. How would you modify it to make it your own?
application_tasks: Create 3 of your own sentences using the core idea of Describing Emotions and Figurative Language Review.
Imagine you are teaching this topic to a friend. Write down exactly what you would say.
challenge_tasks: Combine the concept of 'blithe' with something you learned in a previous chapter to write a short paragraph.
Can you find an exception to this rule or a creative way to bend it? Explain how.
key_points: Use varied adjectives to describe feelings accurately.
Recall and practice alliteration, rhyme, and personification.
Understand idioms as phrases with non-literal meanings.
Recognise the impact of figurative language in writing.

vocabulary.definition: full of energy, excitement, and cheerfulness.
a phrase or expression where the meaning is not obvious from the individual words (e.g., 'kick the bucket' means to die).
